text:
COMBINED TRANSPLANT SURVIVORSHIP / LEUKEMIA CLINIC -- FOLLOW-UP NOTE
12-Month Post-Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Annual Assessment

ATTENDING: Hematology-Oncology / Bone Marrow Transplant
CO-SIGNED: Transplant Survivorship Coordinator

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 49-year-old female presenting for her 12-month post-allogeneic stem cell transplant annual assessment. She was previously treated for ELN Adverse-risk acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD mutation and is now one year out from her matched unrelated donor (MUD) peripheral blood stem cell transplant. She comes in today for surveillance bone marrow evaluation results review, ongoing chronic GVHD reassessment, and long-term survivorship planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo was initially diagnosed with AML approximately 14 months ago after presenting to her primary care physician with progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, spontaneous gingival bleeding, and night sweats. Her initial CBC revealed a white count of 87,400/uL with roughly 42% peripheral blasts, hemoglobin of 7.2, and platelets of 28,000. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML (FAB M1 morphology, 74% blasts by aspirate differential) with trisomy 8 on cytogenetics and subsequently identified high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (allelic ratio 0.91) on molecular profiling. NGS demonstrated a complex co-mutation profile including DNMT3A R882H (VAF 49.8%, presumed founder/CHIP-origin), TET2 Q1299*, WT1 R462W, NRAS Q61H, KDM6A, STAG2, and BCOR mutations. NPM1 was wild-type. ELN 2022 risk: Adverse.

She received induction chemotherapy with 7+3 (cytarabine plus daunorubicin) plus midostaurin (RATIFY protocol). Induction was complicated by E. coli bacteremia during febrile neutropenia, Grade 2 mucositis, and expected transfusion requirements, but no life-threatening complications. Day 35 marrow assessment confirmed complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR. Given her adverse-risk disease profile, she was referred to our transplant program and underwent myeloablative conditioning (fludarabine/busulfan, FluBu4, with PK-adjusted busulfan dosing) followed by peripheral blood stem cell infusion from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (CD34+ dose 6.2 x 10^6/kg). Post-transplant course was notable for Grade 3 mucositis, febrile neutropenia with negative cultures, and mild transaminase elevation without SOS/VOD. Neutrophil engraftment occurred on day +20, platelet engraftment by day +24.

Post-transplant course has been overall excellent. She developed Grade II acute GVHD (Stage 2 skin, Stage 1 GI) at approximately day +47, which responded well to methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with steroid taper over 6 weeks -- no second-line therapy required. She subsequently developed mild chronic GVHD involving skin (xerosis), eyes (sicca features, Schirmer's 5-6 mm bilaterally), and mouth (mild mucosal erythema), managed with topical agents and tacrolimus continuation. Serial marrow assessments at day +30, day +180, and now day +365 have all been MRD-negative with full or near-full donor chimerism. Tacrolimus was discontinued approximately 2 weeks ago (at approximately 11.5 months post-transplant) without any GVHD flare. The peripheral neuropathy she had experienced on tacrolimus (bilateral distal toe tingling, Grade 1) has resolved since discontinuation.

Today she reports significant improvement in her overall quality of life. She has been back to full-time teaching since approximately month 10 post-transplant. She denies new rash, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, or dysphagia. Eye symptoms have substantially improved -- she is still using artificial tears intermittently but no longer finds them bothersome on a day-to-day basis. Dry mouth is largely resolved. She endorses mild residual fatigue at end of the school day, which she feels is continuing to improve. She denies bone pain, joint stiffness, shortness of breath, or chest discomfort. No fevers, no night sweats, no weight loss. She has been exercising lightly (30-minute walks, 4-5 days/week). Appetite is excellent. Sleep is adequate.

Today's visit also includes review of the day +365 surveillance bone marrow biopsy results (performed earlier this week), which are reviewed in detail below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue at end of day, otherwise no fever, chills, or weight loss
- HEENT: Improved dry eyes (using artificial tears as needed, not daily); dry mouth resolved
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest; mild decreased exercise tolerance improving steadily
- GI: Normal bowel habits, no diarrhea, no nausea, no dysphagia
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain, arthralgia, or muscle cramping. DEXA showed mild osteopenia at last assessment; no fragility fractures
- Neurological: Peripheral neuropathy fully resolved after tacrolimus discontinuation. No new neurologic symptoms
- Skin: No active rash; bilateral forearm xerosis fully resolved
- Endocrine: Hypothyroidism -- stable on levothyroxine; no symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism
- Genitourinary: Reports irregular menstrual cycles consistent with post-transplant premature ovarian insufficiency (previously counseled); mild vaginal dryness noted
- Psychiatric: Positive mood overall; finds returning to work highly meaningful. Denies significant anxiety or depression

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 122/74 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 16, Temp 36.8C, SpO2 99% on room air, Weight stable

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Affect bright, engaged.

HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Conjunctivae mildly dry in appearance but no significant injection. Oral mucosa moist and normal in appearance, adequate salivary pooling, no erythema, no ulcerations, no lichenoid changes. No oral thrush.

Lymph nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheeze, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. Bowel sounds normal. No masses.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally. No cyanosis.

Skin: Normal skin color and texture. No rash, no xerosis, no sclerodermatous changes on bilateral forearms or hands. No telangiectasias. No poikiloderma.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities distally. Vibration sense preserved at great toes bilaterally. No ataxia.

Eyes: Schirmer's test performed today: 9 mm/5 minutes right eye, 9 mm/5 minutes left eye (improved from 5-6 mm at month 4). No fluorescein staining performed today given symptomatic improvement.

---

LAB RESULTS (most recent, from this visit and day +365 bone marrow evaluation):

CBC:
- WBC 6,200/uL (differential: 62% neutrophils, 27% lymphocytes, 8% monocytes, 3% eosinophils)
- ANC 3,844/uL
- Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL
- MCV 92 fL
- Platelets 231,000/uL

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- Sodium 138, Potassium 4.1, CO2 24, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- AST 22 U/L, ALT 18 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase 71 U/L
- Albumin 4.3 g/dL

Thyroid function: TSH 2.4 mIU/L (normal, on stable levothyroxine dose)

Tacrolimus trough level: undetectable (as expected; drug discontinued approximately 2 weeks ago)

CMV PCR (peripheral blood): not detected

EBV PCR (peripheral blood): not detected

FSH/LH/estradiol: Elevated FSH (64.2 IU/L) and LH (47.8 IU/L) with low estradiol (18 pg/mL), consistent with chemotherapy/transplant-related premature ovarian insufficiency. Previously discussed; patient counseled regarding genitourinary syndrome of menopause and bone health implications.

---

BONE MARROW BIOPSY / SURVEILLANCE RESULTS (Day +365):

Morphology: Normocellular marrow (cellularity 45-50%) with complete, morphologically normal trilineage hematopoiesis. Myeloblasts less than 2% by morphology -- no atypical myeloid clusters or excess blasts. Erythropoiesis normoblastic and adequate. Megakaryopoiesis with adequate, normal-appearing megakaryocytes.

Flow cytometry MRD (10-color panel, sensitivity 0.01%): NEGATIVE. No aberrant blast immunophenotype detected.

FLT3-ITD PCR (bone marrow and peripheral blood): Not detected, below limit of detection (sensitivity 10^-4).

WT1 quantitative PCR (peripheral blood): 9 copies/10^4 ABL1 copies (well within normal range, less than 50).

DNMT3A R882H allele-specific PCR (peripheral blood): Not detected.

STR Chimerism:
- Overall peripheral blood chimerism: 99.9% donor
- CD3+ T-cell chimerism: 99.2% donor
- CD33+ myeloid chimerism: 100% donor

Conclusion: Complete remission sustained with MRD negativity and full donor chimerism at 12 months post-transplant. All molecular markers of the original AML clone are undetectable.

---

IMAGING:
Surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed at 12 months post-transplant (reviewed separately) showed no evidence of lymphadenopathy, no hepatic or splenic lesions, normal organ sizes, no pulmonary nodules or infiltrates, and no evidence of extramedullary leukemic disease. Previously noted right lower lobe ground-glass opacity (seen at day +90) has been resolved for several months.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 wild-type, ELN Adverse risk) -- STATUS: COMPLETE REMISSION, MRD-NEGATIVE, 12 MONTHS POST-MUD ALLO-HSCT
   This is an outstanding result. Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo achieved CR1 with MRD negativity after a single cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin, proceeded to MUD allo-HSCT in CR1 with FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning, and has now maintained undetectable molecular disease with full donor chimerism at 12 months. All three measurable residual disease markers -- flow cytometry, FLT3-ITD PCR, and WT1 -- remain negative/normal. The DNMT3A R882H founder mutation (which is a CHIP-origin mutation and thus could in theory persist even in the absence of leukemic relapse) is also undetectable, which is a favorable sign, though interpretation of CHIP-level markers post-transplant requires caution. The most common relapse window for FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio AML is within the first 18-24 months post-transplant, so we will continue close surveillance. She is now at low probability of relapse given these results, but not zero.
   - Continue surveillance: peripheral blood chimerism and FLT3-ITD PCR every 3 months through month 24, then every 6 months through year 3
   - Next bone marrow biopsy planned at 18 months post-transplant (month 18) unless earlier clinical concern arises
   - Next combined clinic visit in 3 months
   - Patient counseled at length about symptoms of relapse (new fatigue, fevers, bleeding, weight loss) and instructed to contact clinic urgently if any of these develop

2. Chronic GVHD -- RESOLVED
   Her mild chronic GVHD (skin, eyes, mouth) has fully resolved. Tacrolimus was successfully discontinued at 11.5 months without any flare. Schirmer's test has normalized (9 mm bilaterally). Skin is entirely normal. Oral mucosa normal. NIH Global Chronic GVHD Severity Score: 0 (none). No ongoing immunosuppressive therapy at present.
   - Topical agents (triamcinolone, cyclosporine ophthalmic, artificial tears) discontinued or used only as needed
   - Patient counseled to watch for late GVHD manifestations (bronchiolitis obliterans, esophageal stricture, fasciitis) and to report any new symptoms
   - Off all systemic immunosuppression; infection prophylaxis being further narrowed per protocol

3. Infection prophylaxis -- TRANSITIONING
   Currently off letermovir (stopped day +100). CMV PCR undetectable today. No CMV reactivation events during transplant course.
   - TMP-SMX transitioning to once-daily Monday/Wednesday/Friday dosing; plan to discontinue at month 18 if immune reconstitution adequate
   - Acyclovir to continue through year 2 post-transplant given ongoing immune reconstitution
   - Fungal prophylaxis discontinued per protocol
   - Repeat vaccination series to begin at month 12-18: influenza, pneumococcal, meningococcal, Tdap, and hepatitis vaccines per ASTCT post-transplant immunization schedule

4. Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) / genitourinary syndrome of menopause
   Confirmed by hormonal testing (FSH 64.2, LH 47.8, estradiol 18). Previously counseled. Patient reports mild vaginal dryness.
   - Referral to gynecology for consideration of local (topical) estrogen therapy for genitourinary symptoms; systemic HRT discussion deferred given complexity
   - Ongoing calcium (1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (2000 IU/day) supplementation given osteopenia and POI
   - Repeat DEXA in 2 years; consider bisphosphonate if T-score worsens below -2.5 or fracture occurs

5. Osteopenia (T-score -1.4 lumbar spine, -1.2 femoral neck)
   Related to high-dose steroid exposure during transplant course and post-transplant POI.
   - Calcium/vitamin D supplementation (as above)
   - Encourage weight-bearing exercise (walking program already in place; encourage progression)
   - Monitor per DEXA in 2 years

6. Hypothyroidism -- stable
   TSH 2.4 on current levothyroxine dose. No adjustments needed.

7. Hypertension -- stable
   Blood pressure well-controlled today (122/74). No antihypertensive medication currently required per patient's account (managed with lifestyle). To be continued in primary care.

8. Peripheral neuropathy -- RESOLVED
   Bilateral distal toe tingling fully resolved after tacrolimus discontinuation. No ongoing neuropathy concerns.

9. Long-term survivorship / health maintenance
   - Transitioned back to primary care for routine preventive health screening (cervical, colorectal, breast)
   - Skin cancer surveillance annually given prior immunosuppression
   - Counseled regarding cardiovascular risk reduction: regular aerobic exercise, heart-healthy diet, monitoring lipids and blood pressure in primary care
   - Mental health: patient reports positive mood and finds her return to teaching highly meaningful; no formal referral needed at this time, though open-door for behavioral health resources emphasized
   - Occupational: cleared to continue full-time teaching; no infectious exposure restrictions after month 12 other than standard precautions with sick contacts
   - Second malignancy risk discussed (skin cancers, EBV-related lymphoproliferative disease, therapy-related malignancy) -- lower risk given off immunosuppression; routine surveillance appropriate

ECOG Performance Status: 0

PATIENT EDUCATION AND COMMUNICATION:
Extensive time spent with Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo reviewing all results today. She was clearly very relieved and emotional upon hearing that all markers remain undetectable and that her chimerism is complete. We discussed the difference between remission and cure, emphasizing that while a 12-month MRD-negative, full-chimerism result is very encouraging, continued surveillance is important given the biology of her original disease. She verbalized understanding of relapse warning signs and knows to call the clinic immediately if any concerning symptoms develop. She expressed interest in connecting with an AML survivorship support group, and a referral was provided.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for interval labs and chimerism check.

Time spent with patient: approximately 50 minutes (over 50% counseling and coordination of care).
